concerns.text: The plan treats a Pearson (or Spearman) correlation ≥ 0.8 as evidence of a physical protein‑protein interaction. Correlation alone does not establish physical binding; the construct validity of using a fixed correlation threshold to infer PPIs is weak without additional orthogonal evidence or justification beyond a single citation.

concerns.location: plan.md:## Phase Overview & FR/SC Mapping | Phase 5
concerns.text: The correlation threshold (≥ 0.8) is hard‑coded and later varied in a sensitivity analysis (0.75, 0.80, 0.85). This directly conflicts with FR‑004, which states the threshold must never be set below 0.8, creating an internal inconsistency in the experimental design.

concerns.location: plan.md:## Phase Overview & FR/SC Mapping | Phase 9
concerns.text: Evaluation uses AUROC/AUPRC on a highly imbalanced problem (few true PPIs among millions of gene pairs). The plan does not specify how many negative pairs will be sampled or how class imbalance will be accounted for, risking inflated AUROC scores and insufficient power to detect meaningful differences.

concerns.location: plan.md:## Phase Overview & FR/SC Mapping | Phase 3
concerns.text: Batch‑effect and covariate adjustment are applied only when metadata are present; otherwise the step is a no‑op. This leaves a potential confound uncontrolled for many GEO series that lack complete metadata, undermining the validity of the correlation estimates.

concerns.location: plan.md:## Phase Overview & FR/SC Mapping | Phase 2
concerns.text: TPM normalization is offered as an option, yet TPM is compositional and can induce spurious correlations. Using Pearson correlation on TPM‑scaled data may violate assumptions of linearity and homoscedasticity, threatening construct validity of the co‑expression network.

concerns.location: plan.md:## Phase Overview & FR/SC Mapping | Phase 11
concerns.text: The sensitivity analysis includes a threshold of 0.75, which violates the stated constraint that the correlation threshold must never be set below 0.8. This inconsistency needs resolution before the experiment can be considered methodologically sound.
